paragraphs: Four paired captures, single subject, single room. Each capture pairs raw CSI from a 2-node ESP32-S3 array with continuous heart rate from a Polar H10 chest strap as ground truth.
For each holdout we trained on the remaining sessions and scored the held-out one — a leave-one-session-out evaluation that estimates how the system generalizes to a session it has never seen.

Mean absolute error across the two scored holdouts: 3.89 and 4.41 bpm (mean 4.15). Per-window accuracy within ±5 bpm: 65–68%. PhaseBeat reported 1.5 bpm using $500 Intel 5300 NICs in the same family of methods.
proof.results_link_text: Full methodology, captures, and per-session breakdowns: RESULTS.md
proof.placeholder_caption: [ photo of ESP32-S3 array + result chart goes here ]

meta.title: ViFi — Contactless patient vitals from $44 of WiFi hardware
meta.description: ViFi is building a 510(k)-track contactless patient monitor on commodity ESP32 hardware. 4.15 bpm cross-session HR accuracy on real hardware at ~1/20th the cost of bedside monitors.

open.heading: The code is open.
open.body: ViFi is pre-incorporation. The DSP pipeline, training scripts, API, dashboard, calibration, RF fingerprint, audit log, and 102-test suite all live on GitHub. If you're a researcher, an engineer curious about CSI-based sensing, a press contact, or just want to know whether a $44 sensor really hits 4.15 bpm cross-session — read the code and the methodology.

hospitals.heading: We're recruiting pilot sites.
intro_paragraphs: We're talking to medical/surgical floor leaders, biomedical engineering teams, and clinical research offices interested in hosting a wellness-grade pilot of contactless monitoring.
pilot_bullets: 5–10 rooms instrumented with 2 ESP32-S3 boards each
30–90 day duration, IRB review where applicable
Parallel to your existing standard-of-care monitoring; no clinical decisions are made from ViFi data
Output: a measurement study comparing ViFi against your existing reference

hero.tagline: ViFi is building an FDA 510(k)-track contactless patient monitor that runs on commodity ESP32 chips. We've shown 4.15 bpm cross-session heart-rate accuracy on real hardware against a chest-strap reference — at roughly 1/20th the cost of the bedside monitors hospitals deploy today.

footer.disclaimer_title: ViFi is a pre-incorporation research project.
footer.disclaimer_body: Not yet FDA-cleared. Research and development use only. The current system is not a medical device and must not be used for clinical decision-making.

roadmap.heading: FDA 510(k) Class II contactless vitals monitor.
roadmap.intro: The current system is a pre-clearance research prototype, not a medical device. Our target is FDA 510(k) Class II clearance for heart rate and respiratory rate at roughly 1/20th the per-bed cost of bedside monitors. The path:

steps.title: Multi-subject + multi-room validation
steps.body: 10+ subjects, 3+ rooms, varied HR ranges, paired RR captures with a Vernier respiration belt. Target: cross-subject HR MAE under 3 bpm.

steps.title: Wellness-grade hospital pilot (pre-FDA)
steps.body: 5–10 medical/surgical beds, parallel to existing standard-of-care monitoring, fully de-identified. No clinical decision-making is made from ViFi data during the pilot.

steps.title: FDA 510(k) Class II submission
steps.body: ISO 13485 quality system, clinical validation study at an academic medical center, predicate-device comparison filing.

how_it_works.heading: WiFi packets bend around bodies. We listen to how.
how_it_works.intro: Two ESP32-S3 chips — one transmitting, one receiving — exchange WiFi packets at roughly 70–100 packets per second. Each received packet's per-subcarrier amplitude (Channel State Information, or CSI) reflects the multipath environment in the room, including chest-wall motion from breathing and heartbeat. No line of sight, no contact, no patient compliance.
how_it_works.diagram:
[ESP32-S3 TX] ─────── 192 subcarriers ───────► [ESP32-S3 RX]
   antenna          (chest perturbs path)         antenna
                                                     │
                                                     │  USB serial @ 921600 baud
                                                     ▼
                                            csi_capture.py
                                                     │
                                                     ▼
                                       first_capture_report.py
                                                     │
                                                     ▼
                  HR / RR predictions  vs  Polar H10 ground truth
signal_path_steps: Parabolic peak refinement (cardiac and respiratory bands)
how_it_works.prior_art: The signal-processing approach is from peer-reviewed academic work — PhaseBeat (INFOCOM 2017), FullBreathe (UbiComp 2018), ResBeat (2020). ViFi's contribution is productizing it on commodity ESP32-S3 hardware, with calibration, RF fingerprinting, out-of-distribution suppression, and an auditable prediction pipeline suitable for an FDA submission.

problem.heading: Hospital patients outside the ICU are unmonitored more than 23 hours per day.
paragraphs: On general medical/surgical floors — roughly 700,000 U.S. beds — the standard of care is a vitals reading every 4 to 8 hours by a nurse. Between those readings, transient deteriorations go unobserved: the fever spikes of brewing sepsis, the heart-rate surges of a bacteremia flare, the respiratory depression that precedes an opioid-related code.

Existing continuous-monitoring solutions cost roughly $3,000 per bed and require wires, electrodes, or wearables that patients remove, dislodge, or refuse. They're reserved for the ICU and step-down units. The 80% of beds where most preventable deteriorations actually happen get nothing.
problem.pullquote: ViFi exists because I experienced firsthand the issues with our current system when my mother, who spent 31 days getting diagnosed with Staphylococcus bacteremia after four separate visits, each time with vitals "stable" by the time the nurse arrived.
